epigraph.text:
Remember the former things, those of long ago; I am 
God, and there is no other.

content.text:
We have spent this chapterŌĆöand the two before itŌĆötaking functions apart 
with derivatives. We have asked: given a function $f$, what is $f'$? 
We have used the answer to find tangent lines, rates of change, maximum 
and minimum values, and the shape of graphs.
Now we reverse the question.
*If we know $f'(x)$, can we recover $f(x)$?*

This is not idle curiosity. A physicist who measures acceleration wants 
velocity. An economist who knows the marginal cost wants the total cost. 
A biologist who measures a rate of growth wants the population itself. In 
each case, we know the derivative and we want the original function.
Antiderivatives

content.title: Antiderivative
content.content:
A function $F$ is called an **antiderivative** 
of $f$ on an interval $I$ if
\[
 F'(x) = f(x) \quad\text{for every } x \in I.
\]

content.title: A first antiderivative
content.solution:
We need a function $F(x)$ whose derivative is $2x$. Experience with 
differentiation suggests $F(x) = x^{2}$, since $\deriv{}{x}(x^{2}) = 2x$.

But $F(x) = x^{2} + 5$ also works: $\deriv{}{x}(x^{2} + 5) = 2x$. So 
does $F(x) = x^{2} - 17$, or $F(x) = x^{2} + \pi$. In fact, 
$F(x) = x^{2} + C$ is an antiderivative of $2x$ for *any* 
constant $C$.

content.text:
Are there other antiderivatives we might have missedŌĆöperhaps something 
more exotic than $x^{2} + C$? The answer is no, and the proof uses the 
Mean Value Theorem from [sec:mean-value-theorem].
The $+C$ Theorem

content.title: Antiderivatives Differ by a Constant
content.content:
If $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ on an interval $I$, then the 
**most general** antiderivative of $f$ on $I$ is
\[
 F(x) + C,
\]
where $C$ is an arbitrary constant.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $F$ and $G$ are both antiderivatives of $f$ on $I$. Then 
$F'(x) = f(x) = G'(x)$ for all $x \in I$, so
\[
 (G - F)'(x) = G'(x) - F'(x) = 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
By the corollary to the Mean Value Theorem 
([sec:mean-value-theorem]), a function whose derivative is zero on 
an interval must be constant. Therefore $G(x) - F(x) = C$ for some 
constant $C$, which means $G(x) = F(x) + C$.

content.text:
This is a beautiful example of how theoretical results pay practical 
dividends. The Mean Value TheoremŌĆöwhich may have seemed purely 
abstract in [sec:mean-value-theorem]ŌĆöis the reason we can write 
"$+C$" and be *certain* that we have found *all* 
antiderivatives, not just some of them.
The Integral Sign
We introduce a notation for the general antiderivative.

content.title: Indefinite Integral
content.content:
If $F'(x) = f(x)$, we write
\[
 \int f(x)\dx = F(x) + C
\]
and call this the **indefinite integral** 
of $f$. The symbol $\displaystyle\int$ is the **integral sign**, 
$f(x)$ is the **integrand**, and $C$ is the 
**constant of integration**.

content.text:
The elongated S ($\int$) was introduced by Leibniz in 1675, standing for 
the Latin word *summa* (sum). Its deep connection to summation will 
become clear in Chapter 6; for now, think of $\int f(x)\dx$ simply as 
notation for "the most general antiderivative of $f$."

content.title: Do not forget $+C$
content.content:
Every indefinite integral must include the constant of integration. 
Writing $\int 2x\dx = x^{2}$ is *incomplete*; the correct answer is 
$\int 2x\dx = x^{2} + C$. A missing $+C$ will cost you points on exams 
and, more importantly, can lead to incorrect conclusions in applied 
problems.

content.text: Basic Antiderivative Rules

Every differentiation rule we learned in Chapters 3 and 4 can be read 
"in reverse" to produce an antiderivative rule. Here are the most 
important ones.

content.title: Power Rule for Antiderivatives
content.content:
For any real number $n \neq -1$,
\[
 \int x^{n}\dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This is the reverse of the power rule for derivatives: 
$\deriv{}{x}\!\left(\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}\right) = x^{n}$.

content.title: Applying the power rule
content.problem:
Find each antiderivative.

1. $\displaystyle\int x^{7}\dx = \frac{x^{8}}{8} + C$.

2. $\displaystyle\int \frac{1}{x^{3}}\dx 
 = \int x^{-3}\dx = \frac{x^{-2}}{-2} + C = -\frac{1}{2x^{2}} + C$.

3. $\displaystyle\int \sqrt{x}\dx = \int x^{1/2}\dx 
 = \frac{x^{3/2}}{3/2} + C = \frac{2}{3}x^{3/2} + C$.

content.text:
The case $n = -1$ is special: $\int x^{-1}\dx = \int \frac{1}{x}\dx 
= \ln\abs{x} + C$. We need the absolute value because $\ln x$ is defined 
only for $x > 0$, but $1/x$ is defined for all $x \neq 0$. One can 
verify: $\deriv{}{x}\ln\abs{x} = 1/x$ for both $x > 0$ and $x < 0$.

The remaining basic rules come directly from reversing the derivative 
formulas of Chapter 4.
Two linearity rules allow us to handle sums and constant multiples.

content.title: Linearity of the Indefinite Integral
content.content:
If $F$ and $G$ are antiderivatives of $f$ and $g$, and $k$ is a constant, 
then
$$
 \int [f(x) + g(x)]\dx &= \int f(x)\dx + \int g(x)\dx, 
 \\
 \int k\,f(x)\dx &= k\int f(x)\dx. 
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
In words: the integral of a sum is the sum of the integrals, and constants 
pass through the integral sign. These follow immediately from the 
corresponding rules for derivatives.

content.title: Combining the rules
content.solution:
Apply the sum rule, constant multiple rule, and the basic formulas:
$$
 \int (3x^{4} - 5\sin x + 7)\dx 
 &= 3\int x^{4}\dx - 5\int \sin x\dx + 7\int 1\dx \\
 &= 3\cdot\frac{x^{5}}{5} - 5(-\cos x) + 7x + C \\
 &= \frac{3x^{5}}{5} + 5\cos x + 7x + C.
$$

content.title: Rewriting before integrating
content.solution:
Divide each term by $t^{2}$ first:
\[
 \int \frac{2t^{4} - t^{2} + 3}{t^{2}}\dt 
 = \int \!\left(2t^{2} - 1 + 3t^{-2}\right)\dt 
 = \frac{2t^{3}}{3} - t - \frac{3}{t} + C.
\]

content.text: Initial Value Problems

The general antiderivative contains an arbitrary constant $C$. An 
**initial condition**ŌĆöa known value of the function at a specific 
pointŌĆölets us pin down $C$ and find the *particular* antiderivative 
that satisfies the given condition.

content.title: A basic initial value problem
content.solution:
**Step 1: Antidifferentiate.**
\[
 f(x) = \int (3x^{2} + 2)\dx = x^{3} + 2x + C.
\]

**Step 2: Apply the initial condition.**
\[
 f(0) = 0 + 0 + C = 5 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C = 5.
\]

**Answer:** $f(x) = x^{3} + 2x + 5$.

content.title: From acceleration to position
content.problem:
A ball is thrown upward from a height of $s_{0}$ feet with initial velocity 
$v_{0}$ ft/s. Assuming only gravity acts on it (acceleration 
$a(t) = -32$ ft/s$^{2}$), find the position function $s(t)$.
content.solution:
**Step 1: From acceleration to velocity.**
\[
 v(t) = \int a(t)\dt = \int (-32)\dt = -32t + C_{1}.
\]
Apply $v(0) = v_{0}$: $C_{1} = v_{0}$, so $v(t) = -32t + v_{0}$.

**Step 2: From velocity to position.**
\[
 s(t) = \int v(t)\dt = \int (-32t + v_{0})\dt 
 = -16t^{2} + v_{0}\,t + C_{2}.
\]
Apply $s(0) = s_{0}$: $C_{2} = s_{0}$, so
\[
 s(t) = -16t^{2} + v_{0}\,t + s_{0}.
\]

This is the familiar projectile formula from physicsŌĆöand now we see 
where it comes from: two antidifferentiations starting from constant 
acceleration.

content.text: Looking Ahead

Everything we have done in this section amounts to "running differentiation 
rules in reverse." We can antidifferentiate $x^{n}$, $\sin x$, 
$e^{x}$, and similar functions because we *recognize* them as 
derivatives of known functions. But what about a function defined not by 
a formula but by a physical processŌĆöthe area under a curve, for 
instance?

In Chapter 6, we will discover something astonishing: antiderivatives are 
connected to *area*. The **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** 
unifies the two great operations of calculusŌĆödifferentiation and 
integrationŌĆöinto a single, breathtaking theorem. It is the climax of 
the semester, the theorem toward which every page of this book has been 
building.
We are ready.

marginNotes.text:
The end of a matter is better than its 
beginning, and patience is better than pride.

All your words are true; all your 
righteous laws are eternal.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom 
and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his paths 
beyond tracing out!

For the revelation awaits an appointed 
time; it speaks of the end and will not prove false. Though it linger, 
wait for it; it will certainly come.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646--1716)
marginNotes.text:
It is unworthy of 
excellent men to lose hours like slaves in the labor of calculation which 
could safely be relegated to anyone else if machines were used.

marginNotes.author: Galileo Galilei (1564--1642)
marginNotes.text:
The Book of Nature is written in 
the language of mathematics.
